---
title: "LLM-Based Automated Grading System"
authors:
  - name: Louis L. Odette
    orcid: 0009-0001-8934-6061
    affiliation: 1
  - name: Muhammad Sarim
    orcid: 0009-0008-8751-2514
    affiliation: 2
affiliations:
 - name: University of Windsor School of Business, Sessional Instructor
   index: 1
 - name: University of Windsor School of Business, Teaching Assistant
   index: 2
date: today
format:
  pdf: #latex:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    include-in-header: preamble.tex
    keep-tex: true
editor: visual
bibliography: grading_systems_references.bib
csl: apa.csl
---

<!--
JOSE target budget

Title and authors        not counted
Summary                  120 to 150 words
Statement of Need        180 to 250 words
Educational content      180 to 250 words
Implementation and reuse 150 to 200 words
Example use              100 to 150 words
Acknowledgements          0 to 50 words

Target total             800 to 1000 words
-->

# Summary

This paper presents an open-source, LLM-based system for automatically grading long-form, mixed-format student assignments. The system processes student submissions in batch, returning a grade and written feedback for each question, and is implemented in both R and Python. A JSON rubric schema and companion generation script allow instructors to define structured grading criteria without modifying the underlying code. The software is suited both for self-learning and adoption by instructors who use text-based assignments to assess coding skills and conceptual understanding, and is particularly relevant in higher education settings where class sizes and time constraints make manual grading impractical.

The code and related materials of version 1.0.0 are publicly available [here](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19410580) and licensed under the GPL-3.

# Statement of Need

The code described in this paper was developed in response to constrained grading resources. The authors teach a course of 45–50 students with nine assignments per term, each requiring ten questions to be graded, under a union-imposed cap of three hours per assignment.

Our assignments are a mix of multi-part programming questions in R, open-ended statistics questions that require both conceptual understanding and practical application, and closed-ended and numerical questions, so manual grading would not scale to the class size, given the time constraint.

This is a not uncommon situation [@akyash2025; @qui2025; @on2025], and approaches to the assessment scaling problem have evolved from rule-based and pattern-matching systems for multiple choice questions and simple short-answer questions [@Hussein2019Automated; @Mizumoto2023Exploring; @Ramesh2021An; @Tack2024Automated] through deep-learning and automated code evaluation systems [@Misgna2024A; @Taghipour2016A; @Uto2023Integration], to current sophisticated models leveraging deep learning and large language models (LLMs) [@Beseiso2021A; @ElMassry2025A; @Ren2025Intelligent; @Song2024Automated; @Wang2024EffectivenessOL]. The challenge of assessment is particularly acute for massive open on-line courses (MOOCs) [@on2025].

There are automated grading systems [@floden2025], both commercial \[\@Halgamuge2017\] and open source [@Hamrick2016], though grading systems per se often require adherence to a predefined question and answer framework and corresponding assignment engineering to match the framework. In our course we needed fewer constraints on the form of questions and developed a flexible LLM solution to grade assignments.

LLMs offer greater flexibility than rule-based systems: instructors are not constrained to a fixed question format, and grading criteria can be expressed in natural language rather than code. This makes LLM-based grading a natural fit for courses with mixed assignment types, where questions range from short numerical answers to open-ended programming and conceptual responses.

Despite this promise, usable open-source implementations remain scarce. Most published work stops at the level of prompt examples or informal workflows [@jukiewicz2025; @qui2025; @the2025; @zhao2025], stopping short of a complete, reusable system. We therefore developed our own [@sarim2026].

We built the system around Markdown-based assignments hosted on GitHub. Each student worked in a private repository under a shared course organization, writing answers directly into a Quarto Markdown file alongside any supporting artifacts — data files, images, and PDFs. At the assignment deadline, students committed and pushed their final submission. The instructor then cloned all repositories and ran the grader, which processed every submission in batch. For each question, the grader returned a numeric grade and written feedback. Before releasing grades, the instructor reviewed a sample of graded submissions to check for errors or server-side issues, then returned the results to students.

# Story of the project

This project began as a practical response to an immediate teaching constraint and the system went through several iterations before reaching its current form. Early versions passed only the student submission and the model solution to the LLM, expecting it to infer grading criteria from the contrast between the two. Results were inconsistent: the model would reward partially correct answers generously in one submission and penalize the same error in the next, with no stable basis for comparison across students. Adding an explicit JSON rubric — specifying criteria and point values for each question — was the turning point; grades became reproducible and the feedback became actionable rather than impressionistic.

A second design decision concerned the API architecture. An early implementation used the OpenAI Assistants API, which allowed assignment materials to be uploaded once and retrieved at inference time across a batch of student submissions. This approach reduced token overhead but introduced operational complexity — assistants had to be recreated whenever rubrics or solution files changed — and the architecture is no longer supported in OpenAI's current frontier models. The current system uses the Chat Completions API directly, passing rubric, solution, and student submission together in each request, which is simpler to maintain and model-agnostic. Both implementations are included in the repository, with the Assistants-based version preserved in R for reference.

The system is deployed in a graduate Data Analytics course with 50 students and nine assignments per term. Each assignment carried a three-hour grading budget. Accounting for rubric preparation, batch processing, and instructor review of a sample of submissions, the system kept total grading time within that budget.

No research publications have used the software or have resulted from it.

# Functionality and Usage

## Resources and Documentation:

Full documentation of the application can be found [here](https://github.com/lodette/JOSE_paper/docs).

The codebase provides functionality for grading of student assignments. It deals with varying numbers of assignment questions and can evaluate a mix of programming questions, open-ended statistics questions that require both conceptual understanding and practical application, and closed-ended and numerical questions. In the code, OpenAI's [ChatGPT](https://openai.com/) is used by default and requires a user API key. With the corresponding API keys, [Claude](https://www.anthropic.com/) and [Gemini](https://deepmind.google/models/gemini/) could be used. Both R-centric and Python-centric versions are provided.

Assignments are written in variants of Markdown. The LLM grader is given a folder with all student assignments, where each student's assignment in a separate subfolder, along with an assignment without answers, an assignment with solutions, and a grading rubric in a JSON file. The grader script then processes student submissions in batch.

The output is a CSV file containing, for each student, a numeric grade and written feedback for every question in the assignment. This file is the primary grading artifact: the instructor reviews it — spot-checking a sample of submissions for errors or unexpected scores — before releasing grades to students.

## Documentation

Software documentation is found in */docs/software_documentation.md* and a description of unit tests & GitHub Actions workflows can be found in */docs/ci_testing_overview.md* . The /docs folder also contains separate discriptions of the R and Python pipelines. The document CONTRIBUTION.md explains how to report bugs, ask questions, and contribute code or documentation.

## Example use

### Package Dependencies

Python package dependencies are found in the file *environment.yml*, and R package dependencies are found in the file *renv.lock*.

### Environment variables

Both R and Python versions use an environment variable `OPENAI_API_KEY` to store API credentials. The Python version additionally uses the environment variable `BASE_LAB_DIR` to specify the directory containing the assignment materials and student submissions.

### Usage

The Python version of the grader assumes a folder in the assignments directory is named lab-N, containing the assignments for assignment $N$, and the program is invoked with one of the following commands executed in the terminal:

``` python
python batch_grade.py              # uses default (lab 9)
python batch_grade.py --lab-number 4 # grade lab 4 
python batch_grade.py -n 4 # short form
```

The R version of the grader similarly assumes a folder in the assignments directory is named lab-N, containing the assignments for assignment $N$, and the R program is invoked with the following comands executed in the R Console:

``` r
# Override lab number before calling main()
LAB_NUMBER <- 4
source("R/chat_grading_runner.R")
main()
```

### Rubrics

The grader assumes a JSON file containing the rubric for the current assignment. The rubric is defined by the schema in the file rubric_schema.json, and a draft assignment rubric can be generated using a graded copy of the assignment and the R function R/json_build.R.

## Validity: Performance Against Rubrics

### LLM- performance

After each grading run, the instructor or TA reviewed a sample of approximately five submissions — selected to span the grade range rather than drawn at random — checking each question's numeric score against the written feedback for internal consistency and verifying that the feedback addressed the question actually asked. A score was flagged for correction if the feedback contradicted the assigned points, if the model had graded a blank or malformed response as correct, or if a server-side error had produced a truncated or nonsensical reply. In practice, corrections were infrequent; most runs required no changes. This review step added roughly fifteen minutes to the grading workflow and served as the primary quality gate before grades were released to students.

To evaluate grading consistency, we ran both pipelines on two assignments — lab 4 (10 students) and lab 9 (3 students) — repeating each grading run 50 times per student per pipeline. For each question and student, we computed the standard deviation of the assigned score across runs. Across both assignments and both pipelines, per-question score variance was low: most questions were graded identically on every run, and in no case did the standard deviation exceed 0.25 grade points. To compare the two pipelines, we computed the mean score per question per student for each pipeline and took the absolute difference; R and Python means differed by less than one standard deviation in every case. Variability, where it occurred, was concentrated in questions where student responses were ambiguous or incomplete, suggesting that score variance reflects genuine uncertainty in the submission rather than instability in the grader.

### Performance of Human Graders

A natural question for any automated grading system is how its scores compare to those of human graders. We note, however, that this comparison must be interpreted carefully. In rubric-based assessment, the construct being measured is the degree to which a student response satisfies explicitly stated criteria — not the degree to which it satisfies the implicit expectations of any particular grader. Human graders are known to exhibit substantial inter-rater variability even when applying the same rubric: intraclass correlation coefficients in the range of 0.5–0.8 are typical for short-answer and essay grading tasks (Attali, 2013), and recent work on LLM-based grading has documented scoring disagreements in 65–80% of cases between individual human raters and automated systems, a range comparable to human-human disagreement (Flodén, 2025; Morjaria et al., 2024). An LLM that applies a rubric deterministically will, by construction, achieve perfect self-consistency — a property no human grading team can match. Treating human scores as ground truth therefore conflates rubric fidelity with grader idiosyncrasy, and risks penalizing the automated system for failing to reproduce variance that is, from a measurement standpoint, noise.

We therefore frame the role of human comparison as calibration rather than validation. The relevant question is not whether the system replicates a specific instructor's grades, but whether its interpretation of the rubric falls within the range of scores that competent human graders would assign. Agreement at or above the level of typical inter-rater reliability provides evidence that the system's rubric interpretation is reasonable, while its deterministic consistency offers an advantage that human grading teams cannot provide — particularly in large-enrollment courses where multiple graders introduce additional sources of score variability. A full construct validity study, in which LLM scores are compared against consensus ratings from expert panels rather than individual graders, is beyond the scope of this software paper but is supported by the system's architecture and is a natural direction for future work.

## Strengths, Weaknesses, and Gaps in Automated Grading 

Automated grading encompasses a broad range of approaches. Optical mark recognition (OMR) platforms such as Akindi have effectively solved the problem of scoring fixed-response assessments at scale, offering LMS integration and psychometric feedback including item discrimination and KR-20 reliability indices. Traditional automated essay scoring (AES) engines demonstrated that machine scoring of open-ended text could match human-human agreement levels for constrained writing tasks (Attali, 2013), and recent work using general-purpose LLMs has shown promising alignment with human scores across disciplines without requiring task-specific training data — only a rubric (Flodén, 2025; Morjaria et al., 2024).

However, each tier has significant limitations. OMR systems cannot evaluate constructed responses. AES engines require large prompt-specific training corpora, making them impractical for instructors who write new questions each term. LLM-based approaches address both constraints but introduce concerns around scoring transparency, sensitivity to surface features such as answer length and fluency, and output stochasticity that must be managed through careful parameter control to preserve the consistency advantage automation is meant to provide.

Important gaps remain. Most empirical studies of LLM-based grading treat human scores as ground truth, an assumption that obscures well-documented inter-rater variability and conflates rubric fidelity with grader idiosyncrasy. As we argue above, calibration — verifying that the system's rubric interpretation falls within the range of competent human judgment — is the more appropriate framing. More critically for practitioners, the existing literature is dominated by one-off experimental scripts built for individual studies rather than reusable software designed for adoption. Our system addresses this gap directly: a documented, extensible, open-source tool that allows instructors to deploy LLM-based grading with their own rubrics and integrate it into existing workflows without writing code.

# References {#references .unnumbered}
